title: Psalms 135
subtitle: Praise the Name of the LORD

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — “The LORD Does Whatever Pleases Him”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD does whatever (kōl) pleases him, in the heavens and on the earth, in the seas and all their depths" (v.6). A hallel psalm that summarises Israel’s praise theology. Praise the LORD’s name (vv.1–3). He chose Jacob (v.4). He controls weather, brought Israel out of Egypt, struck nations, gave their land (vv.7–12). The sovereignty declaration of v.6 is the psalm’s theological centre: whatever, wherever — God does as he pleases.

notes.note: "Praise the LORD. Praise the name of the LORD. For the LORD has chosen Jacob to be his own." Election: God chose. Not for Israel’s merit but for God’s pleasure.
"I know that the LORD is great. The LORD does whatever pleases him, in the heavens and on the earth." Total sovereignty: heaven, earth, seas, depths. No domain excepted.

refs.note: A voice from the throne calls all servants to praise God—echoing the psalm's summons.
The LORD chose Israel as his treasured possession because he loved them.
You are a chosen people, a royal priesthood—God's special possession.

hist.context: Psalm 135 opens the Great Hallel (135-136), a major liturgical unit for festival worship. The psalm summons priests and Levites serving in temple courts to praise YHWH. The reason: God chose Jacob/Israel as his treasured possession. This election theology grounds praise—Israel worships because they have been chosen for relationship.

sections.header: Verses 13–21 — “The Idols of the Nations Are Silver and Gold”

heb.paragraph: "The idols (ʿăṣabbîm) of the nations are silver and gold, made by human hands" (v.15). The idol-critique from Psalm 115:4–8 reprised: mouths that don’t speak, eyes that don’t see. "Those who make them will be like them" (v.18). The psalm closes: praise from Zion (v.21). Hallelujah.

notes.note: "The idols of the nations are silver and gold. They have mouths but cannot speak. Those who make them will be like them." The Psalm 115 idol-critique recycled. The warning: you become what you worship.
"Praise the LORD, house of Israel. Praise the LORD, house of Aaron. Praise the LORD from Zion. Hallelujah!" Three houses summoned. One praise from Zion. Final hallelujah.

refs.note: Idols are nothing; there is but one God, the Father, and one Lord, Jesus Christ.
Isaiah's satire on idol-making: craftsmen create gods that cannot save.
The divine being is not like gold or silver images made by human design.

hist.context: The psalm's second movement contrasts YHWH with idols. He does whatever he pleases in heaven and earth; idols have mouths but cannot speak, eyes but cannot see. Those who make them become like them—spiritually dead. The psalm concludes with comprehensive doxology: all Israel (house of Israel, Aaron, Levi) plus God-fearers (perhaps proselytes) are summoned to bless the LORD.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Not for Israel’s merit but for God’s pleasure…

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: For the LORD has chosen Jacob to be his own…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe Lord does whatever pleases him, in the heavens and on the earth, in the seas and all their depths.ESVWhatever the LORD pleases, he does, in heaven and on earth, in the seas and all deeps.NIVHe makes clouds rise from the ends of the earth; he sends lightning with the rain and brings out the wind from his storehouses.ESVHe it is who makes the clouds rise at the end of the earth, who makes lightnings for the rain and brings forth the wind from his storehouses.

rec.text: ""Praise the LORD. Praise the name of the LORD. For the LORD has chosen Jacob to be his own." Election: God chose. Not for Israel’s merit but for God’s pleasure."

rows.label: Idols Again — 135:13–21
rows.text: Silver and gold. Mouths can’t speak. "You become what you worship." Praise from Zion. Hallelujah.

hebtext.note: "The LORD does whatever (kōl) pleases him, in the heavens and on the earth, in the seas and all their depths" (v.6). A hallel psalm that summarises Israel’s praise theology. Praise the LORD’s name (vv.1–3). He chose Jacob (v.4). He controls weather, brought Israel out of Egypt, struck nations, gave their land (vv.7–12). The sovereignty declaration of v.6 is the psalm’s theological centre: whatever, wherever — God does as he pleases.

"The idols (ʿăṣabbîm) of the nations are silver and gold, made by human hands" (v.15). The idol-critique from Psalm 115:4–8 reprised: mouths that don’t speak, eyes that don’t see. "Those who make them will be like them" (v.18). The psalm closes: praise from Zion (v.21). Hallelujah.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: "whatever pleases him." Election of Jacob. The idol-critique repeated. Worship: hallel structure.
